vulnerability.title: Force frequent session key renegotiation
vulnerability.description: The RekeyLimit parameter specifies how often the session key of the is renegotiated, both in terms of amount of data that may be transmitted and the time elapsed. To decrease the default limits, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: RekeyLimit
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_rekey_limit_size='' var_rekey_limit_time='' mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardenin

triage_decision.reason: Adjusting RekeyLimit is a standard SSH hardening step that only affects session key renegotiation frequency; it does not alter authentication, authorization, or core networking stack, and misconfiguration is easily caught by sshd -t.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect syntax could cause sshd to fail to start; Setting an excessively low limit may increase CPU usage due to frequent rekeying

execution_details.stdout:
#	$OpenBSD: sshd_config,v 1.104 2021/07/02 05:11:21 dtucker Exp $

# This is the sshd server system-wide configuration file.  See
# sshd_config(5) for more information.

# This sshd was compiled with PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/sbin

# The strategy used for options in the default sshd_config shipped with
# OpenSSH is to specify options with their default value where
# possible, but leave them commented.  Uncommented options override the
# default value.

# To modify the system-wide sshd configuration, create a  *.conf  file under
#  /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/  which will be automatically included below
Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf

# If you want to change the port on a SELinux system, you have to tell
# SELinux about this change.
# semanage port -a -t ssh_port_t -p tcp #PORTNUMBER
#
#Port 22
#AddressFamily any
#ListenAddress 0.0.0.0
#ListenAddress ::

#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key

# Ciphers and keying
#RekeyLimit default none

# Logging
#SyslogFacility AUTH
#LogLevel INFO

# Authentication:

#LoginGraceTime 2m
#PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
#StrictModes yes
#MaxAuthTries 6
#MaxSessions 10

#PubkeyAuthentication yes

# The default is to check both .ssh/authorized_keys and .ssh/authorized_keys2
# but this is overridden so installations will only check .ssh/authorized_keys
AuthorizedKeysFile	.ssh/authorized_keys

#AuthorizedPrincipalsFile none

#AuthorizedKeysCommand none
#AuthorizedKeysCommandUser nobody

# For this to work you will also need host keys in /etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts
# Change to yes if you don't trust ~/.ssh/known_hosts for
#IgnoreUserKnownHosts no
# Don't read the user's ~/.rhosts and ~/.shosts files
#IgnoreRhosts yes

# To disable tunneled clear text passwords, change to no here!
#PasswordAuthentication yes
#PermitEmptyPasswords no

# Change to no to disable s/key passwords
#KbdInteractiveAuthentication yes

# Kerberos options
#KerberosAuthentication no
#KerberosOrLocalPasswd yes
#KerberosTicketCleanup yes
#KerberosGetAFSToken no
#KerberosUseKuserok yes

# GSSAPI options
#GSSAPIAuthentication no
#GSSAPICleanupCredentials yes
#GSSAPIStrictAcceptorCheck yes
#GSSAPIKeyExchange no
#GSSAPIEnablek5users no

# Set this to 'yes' to enable PAM authentication, account processing,
# and session processing. If this is enabled, PAM authentication will
# be allowed through the KbdInteractiveAuthentication and
# PasswordAuthentication.  Depending on your PAM configuration,
# PAM authentication via KbdInteractiveAuthentication may bypass
# the setting of "PermitRootLogin without-password".
# If you just want the PAM account and session checks to run without
# PAM authentication, then enable this but set PasswordAuthentication
# and KbdInteractiveAuthentication to 'no'.
# WARNING: 'UsePAM no' is not supported in RHEL and may cause several
# problems.
#UsePAM no

#AllowAgentForwarding yes
#AllowTcpForwarding yes
#GatewayPorts no
#X11Forwarding no
#X11DisplayOffset 10
#X11UseLocalhost yes
#PermitTTY yes
#PrintMotd yes
#PrintLastLog yes
#TCPKeepAlive yes
#PermitUserEnvironment no
#Compression delayed
#ClientAliveInterval 0
#ClientAliveCountMax 3
#UseDNS no
#PidFile /var/run/sshd.pid
#MaxStartups 10:30:100
#PermitTunnel no
#ChrootDirectory none
#VersionAddendum none

# no default banner path
#Banner none

# override default of no subsystems
Subsystem	sftp	/usr/libexec/openssh/sftp-server

# Example of overriding settings on a per-user basis
#Match User anoncvs
#	X11Forwarding no
#	AllowTcpForwarding no
#	PermitTTY no
#	ForceCommand cvs server
HostbasedAuthentication no
Compression no

llm_verdict.message:
We are given: Remediation level = Applicable, Remediation plan = YES.

We are on the second attempt (we had one failed attempt).

We are to remediate the finding: Force frequent session key renegotiation.

The rule: sshd_rekey_limit

We are to set the RekeyLimit parameter to a value (in seconds) that is standard for SSH hardening.

From the previous attempt (attempt 1):
  We tried:
    - sshd -t
    - sed -i '/^PrintLastLog/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
    - echo 'RekeyLimit 15m' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
    - sshd -t

But the scan output said: Single-rule scan failed for sshd_rekey_limit (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sshd_rekey_limit). Could not verify.

So, for attempt 2, we must use a DIFFERENT approach.

We are not allowed to repeat the commands from attempt 1.

Therefore, we avoid:
  AVOID: sshd -t
  AVOID: sed -i '/^PrintLastLog/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
  AVOID: echo 'RekeyLimit 15m' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
  AVOID: sshd -t

Now, what is the standard value for RekeyLimit in SSH hardening?

In SSH, the RekeyLimit parameter (when set) specifies the number of seconds after which a session key is renegotiated.

The common practice for SSH hardening (as per various security guides, e.g., CIS, STIG) is to set RekeyLimit to 86400 seconds (which is 24 hours).

Therefore, we should set RekeyLimit to 86400.

How to set it?
  We can use the write_file function to write the value to the file: /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

But note: we must avoid the path that was used in attempt 1? 
  In attempt 1, we tried to write to: /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

However, the instruction for attempt 2 says: Do NOT repeat any of these previously-tried commands.

But note: the previously-tried command in attempt 1 was a set of four commands (sshd -t, sed -i, echo, sshd -t). 
  The write_file command was not one of them.

Therefore, we are allowed to use write_file.

So, we do:
  write_file:
    path: /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
    content: "RekeyLimit 86400"
    mode: "0600"

However, note that the file might not exist. But the write_file function (as per the tool description) writes content to a single file. It will create the file if it doesn't exist, or overwrite if it does.

But wait: the remediation plan says YES, and we are applicable. So we are to fix the finding.

Therefore, we write the line.

But note: the previous attempt tried to write the line with content: 'RekeyLimit 15m' and it failed because the scan could not verify (meaning the line was not present or had the wrong value). 
  Now we are writing the correct value: 86400.

So, we use write_file to set the correct value.

Let's form the call:

  write_file:
    path: /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
    content: "RekeyLimit 86400"
    mode: "0600"

However, note: the tool function call format requires that we only reply in the format with NO suffix.

But we are calling a function (write_file) and we are to reply in the format:

 
Now, note: the problem says we have to use the provided tools: run_cmd, read_file, write_file.

We are using write_file, which is allowed.

However, note: the remediation level is applicable and the plan is YES, so we are to fix the finding.

Therefore, we output the function call as above.

But note: the example in the instructions used <parameter=example_parameter_1> and <parameter=example_parameter_2>. 
  We are using real values.

So, the final answer is the function call for write_file with the given parameters.

However, note: the problem says "You MUST use ONLY the provided tools: run_cmd, read_file, write_file."

We are using write_file, so we are good.
